summary:
Claim: Anti-HBc negativity does not exclude active hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection.  
Evidence: - In early phases of acute HBV infection, anti-HBc antibodies may be absent despite persistent HBsAg positivity. - Technical limitations or assay variability can lead to false-negative results for anti-HBc.  
Answer: B

This conclusion aligns with current guidelines that emphasize direct detection of HBV DNA over indirect serologic markers when assessing infectiousness and treatment eligibility. The presence of HBsAg alone is insufficient to determine whether there is ongoing viral replication; only HBV DNA testing confirms active viremia. Therefore, initiating further evaluation through HBV DNA quantification ensures accurate risk stratification and appropriate clinical intervention—especially in patients where treatment decisions depend on viral load rather than serology alone.

(498 tokens)

Note: Option B refers to "Test for HBV DNA" as the recommended next step.
